text: What is the output of this code?
explanation: Stream.generate(() -> "") is an infinite stream of empty strings. filter(notEmpty) keeps only non-empty strings, but there are none — so the stream runs forever trying to find a match.
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 10 – Streams, pp. 449–453

text: What is the result of the following code?
explanation: iterate(1, n->n+1) generates 1,2,3,4,5,… limit(5) gives 1,2,3,4,5. filter even keeps 2,4. sum = 6.
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 10 – Streams, pp. 430–436

text: Which functional interface best represents the method signature: String apply(String s)?
explanation: Function<T,R> maps T → R. UnaryOperator<T> extends Function<T,T> (same input and output type). Both B and E are technically correct, but Function<String,String> is the more general answer. UnaryOperator<String> is also valid — if both options appeared, choose E.
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 9 – Lambdas and Functional Interfaces, pp. 403–408

text: What is the output of the following code?
explanation: Optional.of("hello") wraps "hello". map(toUpperCase) → Optional.of("HELLO"). filter(length > 3): "HELLO" has length 5 > 3, so the Optional is still present. orElse() returns the wrapped value "HELLO" since it is present.
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 10 – Streams, pp. 459–465

text: What is the output of the following code?
explanation: sorted() orders alphabetically: four, one, three, two. limit(2) takes the first two: four, one. forEach prints them in encounter order.
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 10 – Streams, pp. 443–449

text: What is the output of the following reduce operation?
explanation: reduce(identity, accumulator) starts with 0 and applies (a, b) -> a + b cumulatively: 0+1=1, 1+2=3, 3+3=6, 6+4=10, 10+5=15.
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 10 – Streams, pp. 453–458

text: Which of the following are terminal operations on a Stream? (Choose all that apply.)
explanation: Terminal operations produce a result or side effect and consume the stream: collect(), count(), findFirst(), anyMatch(), forEach(), reduce(), etc. Intermediate operations (filter, map, sorted, etc.) return a new Stream and are lazy.
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 10 – Streams, pp. 430–433

text: What is the output of the following code?
explanation: flatMap flattens the nested lists into a single stream: 1,2,3,4,5. filter(n > 2) keeps 3,4,5. count() returns 3.
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 10 – Streams, pp. 436–439

text: What is the output of the following Collectors.groupingBy code?
explanation: groupingBy(String::length) groups strings by their length. Length 2 includes "bb" and "dd". result.get(2) returns [bb, dd].
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 10 – Streams, pp. 465–472

text: What is the output of the following primitive stream code?
explanation: IntStream.range(1, 6) produces 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 (exclusive end). mapToObj(i -> i*i) converts to boxed stream of squares: 1, 4, 9, 16, 25. forEach prints them.
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 10 – Streams, pp. 472–478

text: Which of the following are true about Optional in Java 21? (Choose all that apply.)

options.text: get() on an empty Optional throws NoSuchElementException.
orElseThrow() is equivalent to get() in terms of behavior.

explanation: Optional.of(null) throws NullPointerException. ofNullable(null) creates empty Optional. get() and orElseThrow() both throw NoSuchElementException on empty Optional (orElseThrow() is the preferred, more descriptive name).
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 10 – Streams, pp. 459–465

text: What does Collectors.joining(", ", "[", "]") produce for Stream.of("a", "b", "c")?
explanation: Collectors.joining(delimiter, prefix, suffix) joins elements with the delimiter and wraps with prefix and suffix. joining(", ", "[", "]") gives [a, b, c].
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 10 – Streams, pp. 465–472

text: What is the output of the following code?
explanation: Stream.concat(s1, s2) creates a single stream of s1 elements followed by s2 elements: a,b,c,d. map(toUpperCase) converts to A,B,C,D. forEach prints without separator: ABCD.
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 10 – Streams, pp. 430–433

text: Which of the following statements about lazy evaluation in streams are correct? (Choose all that apply.)

options.text: Intermediate operations are not executed until a terminal operation is called.
Calling filter() on a stream immediately starts processing elements.
A stream can be used as many times as needed.
Short-circuit operations like findFirst() can stop processing early.
The order of intermediate operations can affect performance.

explanation: Streams are lazy — intermediate operations are only executed when a terminal operation is invoked. filter() and map() do not process elements immediately. Once a stream is consumed, it cannot be reused. Short-circuit operations like findFirst() and anyMatch() can terminate before processing all elements. Placing filter() before map() reduces unnecessary transformations.
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 10 – Streams, pp. 430–436

text: What is the output of the following code?
explanation: IntStream.average() returns an OptionalDouble. The average of 2, 4, 6, 8 is (2+4+6+8)/4 = 20/4 = 5.0. getAsDouble() returns the double value.
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 10 – Streams, pp. 472–478
